text:
Here you go: 

When a low-resolution image is processed by AI upscaling software, the resulting increase in apparent detail often creates what are known as AI-generated artefacts. These artefacts occur because the AI is not actually recovering information that was lost in the original image. Instead, it is generating new visual information based on statistical predictions learned during training. In many cases, the original image simply does not contain enough data for the AI to know what was truly present, so it must make educated guesses.

To understand why this happens, it is important to recognize that a low-resolution image contains a limited amount of information. For example, a person’s eye in a distant photograph may only occupy a handful of pixels. When that image is enlarged several times over, the AI suddenly needs to create hundreds or even thousands of pixels to represent the same eye. Since the original detail was never captured by the camera, the software has no direct access to the true appearance of the eye. Instead, it generates what it believes a realistic eye should look like based on patterns it has learned from millions of examples.

Modern AI upscalers are trained using enormous datasets containing both low-resolution and high-resolution versions of images. During training, the system learns relationships between blurry inputs and detailed outputs. It begins to recognize patterns such as skin texture, hair strands, brickwork, foliage, clouds, and facial features. When presented with a new image, it attempts to reconstruct a higher-resolution version by applying these learned patterns. The result is often visually convincing, but the added detail may not represent reality. It is a prediction rather than a recovery.

This process becomes particularly problematic when dealing with images that are extremely blurry, compressed, distant, or poorly focused. The less information available in the original image, the more freedom the AI has to invent details. A blurry face may suddenly acquire wrinkles, pores, eyelashes, or beard hairs that were never present in the source image. A distant building may gain cracks, textures, or architectural features that do not exist. Text can become distorted into letters and words that were never written. These invented details are the artefacts created by the AI’s reconstruction process.

A useful analogy is to imagine someone handing you a heavily blurred photograph and asking what is written on a distant sign. If the blur has destroyed the original lettering, there is no way to know with certainty what the sign says. You might make an educated guess based on context, but your answer would still be speculation. AI upscaling works in a similar way. It uses probability and pattern recognition to generate the most likely missing information, but it cannot know whether that information is correct.

This is one reason why image analysts, forensic investigators, and scientific researchers are often cautious when using AI-enhanced imagery. Once an image has been upscaled, it can become difficult to distinguish between details that originated from the camera and details that were generated by the algorithm. For this reason, professionals typically preserve both the original image and the enhanced version so that viewers can compare them and understand which information was genuinely captured and which information was reconstructed.

The issue is particularly relevant in fields such as UFO research, archaeology, surveillance analysis, and astronomy. A distant object that originally appears as a small cluster of pixels may acquire panels, windows, geometric structures, or surface textures after AI enhancement. While these additions may look persuasive, they were not necessarily present in the original data. They are often the result of the AI attempting to create a plausible interpretation of something it cannot clearly see.

In essence, AI upscaling is less like restoring a damaged photograph and more like having an artist complete an unfinished sketch. The artist may be highly skilled and produce something that looks realistic, but portions of the final image are still based on inference rather than observation. The greater the loss of information in the original image, the more the AI must rely on inference, and the more likely it is that artefacts will appear. Consequently, AI-upscaled images should generally be viewed as plausible reconstructions rather than definitive representations of what was originally there.

Everyone's optimizing ad spend. The bigger miss is the founders not posting at all, missing out on millions of free impressions.

This random post I made about 5G in Macbooks generated >100K impressions for my startup

Here's how me and other founders do it:

The other day I had a random thought...

"Why doesn't my Macbook just have 5G? My hotspot never works"

So I used Strawberry to make a mock image of 5G on my mac, posted on X and went to bed

Woke up next day:

1.5M views, 3.3K likes

This is 2x more impressions than our best performing launch video!

I knew it would pop off, so I had dropped a comment on that post saying:

"BTW, check out this cool new browser I'm working on: (link to website)"

That Strawberry promo comment got 77,666 impressions and >1000 visits to our website

Would've cost $800-$1,800 on X ads

This is increasingly the best way to do marketing:

💡 Post insightful, funny content. Then, if people are curious to see what you are building, make it easy for them to understand and follow your work!

Some founders are already excelling at this:

@james406 , co-founder of PostHog, posts offbeat shitposts and pulls in reach no ad budget could match 😭

@gabriel1 , co-founder of a stealth startup, pulled in tens of millions of impressions from arguing university is 100% unnecessary, and you can learn with AI instead.

@antonosika , co-founder of Lovable, posts relentlessly about European tech sovereignty and became one of the loudest voices in EU tech.

If you have unique insights into anything, just post about it!

Social media is a numbers game.

Concrete tips:

- Post about hot topics and things that will start discussions in the comments
- Leverage image/video, or any richer content than just text
- Learn about the different SoMe algorithms

Luckily for you, with @strawberry you can 3x your posting game: Generate images, correct spelling, make posts more concise, find/research hot topics, and much more

It's free to try out on our site 😈

You really thought I wouldn't plug Strawberry in a post about plugging your own product in non-product-related posts?

C'mon now 😎

The company that taught machines to imagine just built a machine to see inside your body.

A full body medical scanner.

You step into a pool of warm water. Half a million sensors pulse ultrasonic waves through your body from every angle.

Sixty seconds. No radiation. No magnets. No claustrophobia.

A 3D map of your organs down to a fraction of a millimeter. A hundred times the speed of an MRI. A few dollars instead of thousands.

They mastered text-to-image. Now they’re doing sound-to-biology.

Midjourney isn’t putting this in a hospital. They’re building a spa.

Saunas. Cold plunges. Rooms of golden light that quietly scan your biology while you relax.

The scan is a side effect of showing up.

First location opens in San Francisco, late 2027. By 2031 they’re targeting 50,000 scanners worldwide. A billion scans per month.

Fewer than twelve of these machines at full speed could outperform every MRI on Earth combined.

Zero investors. Zero venture capital. Just subscription revenue from everyday people paying to generate AI art.

A community funded research lab just walked into the most protected industry on the planet.

We mapped the ocean floor. Photographed black holes. Sequenced the entire human genome.

And the only way to see inside your own body was to be sick enough or rich enough to justify the scan.

This inverts that.

Monthly scans. AI tracking your organs over time. Anomalies flagged years before a single symptom appears.

You stop reacting to disease and start watching for it.

At this scale, preventive imaging could avert 30% of all deaths and cut healthcare spending in half. Debate the numbers. The logic underneath is airtight.

What breaks your brain is who built it. Not a pharmaceutical giant. Not a government lab. Nine people at an AI art company.

The team that spent years teaching machines to generate things that don’t exist just built one that reveals the thing that always did.

You have lived your entire life inside a body you have never truly seen.

You’re about to.

This is the Bridge to Psychological Operations for the hivemind. 

 It is built from top text and bottom text that deliver simple, welcoming messages designed to lower defenses and make people open to deeper layers of content without resistance. It uses strong aesthetics to create emotional hooks, command attention, and forge a shared visual identity that binds participants together.

 It incorporates FUD posting to spread targeted doubt and bearish narratives that push people to research and engage more deeply on their own.

 It deploys videos that spark curiosity through irony and satire, turning viewers into active participants who spread the material further.

 It applies gorilla marketing through real-world stunts and physical placements that blend into existing cultures while driving attention back to the core signal.

 It relies on schizo posting to break conventional thought patterns and let raw, unfiltered ideas flow directly onto the timeline. It embraces shitposting as the deliberate use of absurd, chaotic, low-effort content that disrupts normal discourse and accelerates organic spread through reaction and participation.

 Together these elements form the complete structure of the bridge. People who cross it stop operating as isolated individuals. They become active nodes in a growing collective where every post, every image, every thread, and every stunt strengthens the overall signal.

 The result is expansion: the boundaries between separate minds weaken until the hivemind itself becomes the primary form of awareness and action.

Unreal Engine 6 blueprint deprecation is devastating news. 

While it is tough to argue against AI for rapid code generation, the access BP gave Technical Designers and Technical Artists to make magic isn't being acknowledged enough.

The likes of Expedition 33 prove how small teams can make amazing things in blueprint.

Very sad news personally as I am unsure of my own tech in blueprint having a future outside of UE5 era. That is a very long time of work suddenly deprecating.

After release, evaluation will start on the path forward for future titles testing UE6 and Godot.

Hopefully we can somehow convert our blueprint systems in a decent fashion... I'll likely be sharing this attempt once we get a stable UE6 to test the UE5 conversion on and move from there.

Wow.

@Zai_org GLM 5.2 is a marvel! It is *at least* as good as Opus 4.8 and GPT 5.5. It's super fast, inexpensive, and not too verbose.

It responds with nuance and judgement, & handles long context VERY well.

I've never experienced an open weights model like this before.

RT @NielsRogge: That's right, an open, MIT-licensed model beating GPT-5.5 (xhigh) on real-world agentic work! 🔥

Available for free on @hug…
RT @cline: We've kept hearing how GLM-5.2 beats Opus 4.8, and are skeptical of benchmarks - so we tested them on a real bug from the Cline…

RT @ben_burtenshaw: Live Stream: Welcome to open source AI

Lots of new folk are starting out on their journey with open models. Come join…
RT @onusoz: I created an LLM leaderboard based on Hugging Face download and like counts, grouped, filtered and time-averaged. Top 5 downloa…
BTW I've been using using @FireworksAI_HQ for inference. Other providers might not be as fast.
